content.description: Parked events in S3 flagged as a new alarm — Michal explained two parking strategies exist: dead letter queue for infrastructure failures, S3 for code-level errors — thresholds need fine-tuning since the connector merged ~one week ago.
Email collisions account for ~20% of users and were always expected; Jennifer confirmed these parked events don't need alerting and should be cleared, not manually handled.
Categorizing all parked event errors is the agreed approach — each bucket needs triage to determine which errors to delete, ignore, or escalate, with findings feeding into a playbook.
Francis suggested creating granular, folder-level alerts for each S3 bucket type rather than one aggregate alarm, enabling clearer identification of critical vs. non-critical events.

SMS delivery alert investigated: at a 5-minute period, failure rates appeared very high, but extending the period to 6 hours showed ~93–98% delivery — low message volumes were skewing short-period percentages.
Francis noted that in the SMS industry, a ~10% failure rate is acceptable; invalid, non-SMS-eligible, or incorrect phone numbers are a common cause, especially at low volumes.

Anomaly detection proposed as a better approach for the SMS alarm vs. a fixed threshold, with the option to combine both methods; Francis took ownership of investigating the best configuration based on available data.

Event bus alarm identified as misconfigured — likely triggering on missing data since December, never resetting because alarms only fire on state transitions; Michal confirmed it needs investigation and a fix.
Despite the misconfiguration, Jennifer noted her dashboards show events are still flowing daily, so this is not a P1, but still warrants prompt attention.

Priority debate resolved: Jennifer argued alarms must reach net zero before broader team involvement, since constant noise has caused important alerts to be ignored — Wesley agreed to follow her direction.

Sentry vs. CloudWatch duplication flagged as a follow-on concern — Sentry should focus on intelligent insights rather than duplicating CloudWatch alerts; this is lower priority than clearing current alarm noise.
Cognito-related alarms already have Jira tickets and are tracked in Sentry; once persistent alarms are resolved, noisy intermittent ones will become more visible and can be ticketed individually.

data.raw_meeting:
**Mandalore, AWS Alert Planning**
🕞 Started at 01:15 PM on 26 Mar, lasted 45m
data.raw_content:
- Parked events in S3 flagged as a new alarm — Michal explained two parking strategies exist: dead letter queue for infrastructure failures, S3 for code-level errors — thresholds need fine-tuning since the connector merged ~one week ago. 
- Email collisions account for ~20% of users and were always expected; Jennifer confirmed these parked events don't need alerting and should be cleared, not manually handled. 
- Categorizing all parked event errors is the agreed approach — each bucket needs triage to determine which errors to delete, ignore, or escalate, with findings feeding into a playbook. 
- Francis suggested creating granular, folder-level alerts for each S3 bucket type rather than one aggregate alarm, enabling clearer identification of critical vs. non-critical events. 
- SMS delivery alert investigated: at a 5-minute period, failure rates appeared very high, but extending the period to 6 hours showed ~93–98% delivery — low message volumes were skewing short-period percentages. 
- Francis noted that in the SMS industry, a ~10% failure rate is acceptable; invalid, non-SMS-eligible, or incorrect phone numbers are a common cause, especially at low volumes. 
- Anomaly detection proposed as a better approach for the SMS alarm vs. a fixed threshold, with the option to combine both methods; Francis took ownership of investigating the best configuration based on available data. 
- Event bus alarm identified as misconfigured — likely triggering on missing data since December, never resetting because alarms only fire on state transitions; Michal confirmed it needs investigation and a fix. 
- Despite the misconfiguration, Jennifer noted her dashboards show events are still flowing daily, so this is not a P1, but still warrants prompt attention. 
- Priority debate resolved: Jennifer argued alarms must reach net zero before broader team involvement, since constant noise has caused important alerts to be ignored — Wesley agreed to follow her direction. 
- Sentry vs. CloudWatch duplication flagged as a follow-on concern — Sentry should focus on intelligent insights rather than duplicating CloudWatch alerts; this is lower priority than clearing current alarm noise. 
- Cognito-related alarms already have Jira tickets and are tracked in Sentry; once persistent alarms are resolved, noisy intermittent ones will become more visible and can be ticketed individually.
